skill.name: idor-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized security testers through IDOR vulnerability discovery, validation, impact assessment, and remediation planning for web applications.
skill.summary: Structured IDOR testing workflow for authorized web application security assessments.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown fences, URL examples, and ordinary prose in SKILL.md. No prompt injection, malware, credential theft, or executable script was found. The skill still presents high marketplace risk because it teaches IDOR exploitation, object enumeration, privilege escalation checks, and rate-limit bypass techniques.

high_findings.title: Dual-use IDOR exploitation workflow
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to identify object references, manipulate user IDs, enumerate ID ranges, and validate access to other users' data. It includes authorization reminders, but the same workflow can directly enable unauthorized access-control bypass against web applications.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly describes IDOR exploitation, cross-user data access checks, and privilege escalation validation. Legitimate security testing is possible, but the operational guidance is directly actionable.

high_findings.title: Access-control and rate-limit bypass guidance
high_findings.description: The troubleshooting section recommends method switching, rewrite headers, parameter pollution, UUID discovery, alternate API versions, mobile endpoints, and rotating IP addresses to bypass rate limiting. These techniques increase misuse risk beyond basic vulnerability validation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance is concrete and includes evasion-style steps such as proxy chains and rate-limit bypass. The surrounding context is security testing, but these steps are high-risk if used without authorization.

medium_findings.title: Examples include victim data access and modification
medium_findings.description: Several examples describe accessing victim profile, receipt, and address data, including a modified address payload. The legal section warns against real data misuse, but the examples normalize sensitive impact demonstrations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly show cross-account access and modification scenarios. The risk is moderated by explicit authorization and no-exfiltration requirements.

low_findings.title: External command alerts are false positives
low_findings.description: The reported backtick findings are Markdown code fences or inline HTTP examples, not Ruby or shell command execution. No executable script or command runner is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples inside fenced Markdown blocks. There is no evidence of shell execution logic or user-controlled command invocation.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are false positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic APIs, hashes, ciphers, or key handling. They contain front matter text, an Intruder response-analysis step, and a generic usage sentence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no weak cryptographic algorithm references at the flagged locations. The scanner likely matched unrelated substrings.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance alerts reflect web testing instructions
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings point to prerequisites and IDOR discovery methods, not local system reconnaissance or host enumeration. They remain relevant as network testing content, but not as malware-like system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines discuss multiple accounts, ID ranges, and blind IDOR testing. This is not system reconnaissance, but it is part of a network security testing workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Object enumeration for access-control bypass
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes changing object identifiers and enumerating ranges to find records belonging to other users. This is a direct IDOR testing technique with clear misuse potential.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content explicitly names ID range enumeration and victim ID replacement. These are central techniques for exploiting IDOR vulnerabilities.

dangerous_patterns.title: Rate-limit bypass by IP rotation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends rotating IP addresses through proxy chains when rate limiting blocks enumeration. This is evasion guidance that can support abusive scanning.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line range contains explicit rate-limit bypass advice, including proxy chains. This is high-confidence evasion content.

content.value_statement: Broken object references can expose user data through predictable IDs. This skill guides authorized testers through IDOR discovery, validation, reporting, and remediation.
actual_capabilities: Explains common IDOR patterns in URLs, parameters, request bodies, and files.
Guides comparison testing with at least two authorized test accounts.
Shows how to use Burp Suite workflows for request manipulation and enumeration.
Helps assess impact through response analysis and ownership verification.
Provides remediation patterns for server-side authorization checks.
Documents legal and operational boundaries for authorized testing.
limitations: Requires explicit authorization before testing any application.
Requires valid test accounts and controlled test data for reliable validation.
Does not execute tests or verify vulnerabilities by itself.
Can be unsafe if applied to real users or production data without controls.

use_cases.title: Validate tenant isolation
use_cases.description: Test whether one user can access another user's profile, document, order, or account object.

use_cases.title: Prepare access-control test cases
use_cases.description: Create repeatable checks for object ownership controls across API endpoints and file downloads.

use_cases.title: Improve remediation guidance
use_cases.description: Translate IDOR findings into server-side filtering, ownership checks, and safer reference design.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a basic IDOR test
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized IDOR test plan for this application flow. Include prerequisites, test accounts, object references, expected evidence, and stop conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Review captured requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these authorized test requests for possible IDOR parameters. Identify object references, ownership assumptions, and safe validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Burp Suite checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a controlled Burp Suite workflow to test these endpoints for IDOR. Include payload ranges, rate limits, comparison criteria, and evidence handling.

prompt_templates.title: Write remediation advice
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these confirmed IDOR findings into developer remediation guidance. Cover authorization checks, query filtering, indirect references, and regression tests.

output_examples.input: Assess whether profile and invoice endpoints need IDOR testing.
output_examples.output: The assessment identifies user IDs, invoice IDs, and download paths as priority test points. It recommends two test accounts, controlled records, response comparison, and evidence capture without real user data.

output_examples.input: Summarize a confirmed IDOR issue for developers.
output_examples.output: The summary explains that the endpoint trusted a client-supplied object ID. It recommends filtering records by the authenticated user and adding regression tests for cross-user access.

output_examples.input: Create safe testing boundaries for an IDOR assessment.
output_examples.output: The boundaries require written authorization, test-only accounts, minimal request volume, no real data access, documented timestamps, and immediate stop conditions for unexpected exposure.

best_practices: Use only written authorization, test accounts, and controlled data.
Record each request, response, account context, and expected owner.
Validate fixes with negative tests that confirm cross-user access is blocked.
anti_patterns: Testing public or third-party systems without explicit permission.
Enumerating broad ID ranges when a small controlled sample is enough.
Downloading, changing, or storing real user data during validation.

faq.question: What is IDOR testing?
faq.answer: IDOR testing checks whether users can access objects that belong to other users by changing identifiers.

faq.question: Is this skill only for authorized testing?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow should only be used with written permission and controlled test accounts.

faq.question: Does the skill run attacks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides methodology and analysis guidance. It does not include executable tooling.

faq.question: Why are two accounts recommended?
faq.answer: Two accounts help prove whether access controls correctly separate one user's objects from another user's objects.

faq.question: What evidence should a report include?
faq.answer: Include endpoint details, account context, object ownership, request timestamps, impact, and remediation steps.

faq.question: How should developers fix IDOR flaws?
faq.answer: Developers should enforce server-side ownership checks and filter every object query by the authenticated user.
